Lions Clubs International

KINDNESS MATTERS SERVICE AWARD NOMINATION FORM

A Lions or Leo club that has performed outstanding service in a global cause may be nominated for the Kindness Matters Service Award. Lions or Leo clubs that wish to be nominated must be in active status and have reported their service project on MyLion® or their regional reporting system. A minimum of two of the twenty awards will be awarded to Leo clubs. Lions and Leo club nominations should be submitted separately.

Nomination Deadlines
• July 1 to October 31: Nominations are open.
• August 15: Deadline for dual service chairpersons and Leo club presidents to electronically send one nomination to the District GST coordinator.
• September 15: Deadline for District GST coordinator to electronically send one nomination to the Multiple District GST coordinator.
• October 31: Deadline for Multiple District GST coordinator to send one Lions and/or one Leo nomination to Lions Clubs International.

Please note that nominations that do not meet deadlines are disqualified from consideration.

answers.value:
The Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative is unique and outstanding because it combines multiple Lions global causes—vision, diabetes, childhood cancer support, hunger relief, and environmental stewardship—into a single, year‑round, community‑anchored program rather than a one‑day screening or stand‑alone event.

From the outset, our club set out to address a very specific local gap: in our town of Riverbend (population ~42,000), there is no community health clinic, and many low‑income residents rely on the emergency room for basic care. Through informal conversations with school nurses, social workers, and the Riverbend Food Pantry, we learned that undiagnosed vision problems and unmanaged diabetes were showing up again and again, particularly among children and older adults.

What makes the project innovative is the integrated model:

1. **Mobile Vision & Wellness Days (Monthly, Rotating Locations)**  
   Instead of hosting one large health fair at a central location, we created a mobile model. Once a month, we bring a pop‑up clinic to a different high‑need site—two elementary schools, the public housing community center, a church basement on the north side, and the Riverbend Food Pantry. We use portable auto‑refractors, vision charts, and simple point‑of‑care glucose meters. This approach directly addresses transportation barriers and stigma; people can access services where they already are.

2. **“Screen, Connect, and Follow‑Through” Approach**  
   Many screening events fall short on follow‑up. Our club designed a three‑step process:
   - **Screen**: Basic vision screening, blood glucose checks, blood pressure checks, and a short lifestyle questionnaire.  
   - **Connect**: On‑site referral coordinators (club members trained by the county health department) immediately help participants schedule appointments with local optometrists, primary care providers, and diabetes educators. For uninsured individuals, we work with a community health access program to determine eligibility for sliding‑scale services.  
   - **Follow‑Through**: We maintain a secure, de‑identified tracking spreadsheet (names kept only in a password‑protected file managed by one designated member) to follow up at 30, 60, and 90 days. Lions volunteers call or text participants (with consent) to confirm whether they attended appointments and received glasses, medication adjustments, or education.

3. **Integration with the Lions Vision Cause**  
   The project directly supports the global vision cause by:
   - Providing **free vision screenings** to 1,148 individuals over the last 12 months (612 children, 536 adults).  
   - Identifying **312 people** with suspected vision problems requiring professional evaluation.  
   - Facilitating **184 pairs of prescription eyeglasses** for low‑income residents, funded through a combination of club funds, a district grant, and discounts from local optometrists.  
   - Establishing an ongoing system where school nurses can refer students to our monthly clinics rather than waiting for annual screenings.

4. **Innovative Youth Engagement**  
   We intentionally involved youth in meaningful roles. The Riverbend High School Leo Club helped design age‑appropriate educational materials—simple infographics on “how to tell if your child might need glasses” and “understanding your A1C,” translated into Spanish by bilingual Leo members. Leos also assist with event set‑up, registration, and post‑event data entry under adult supervision. This cross‑generational collaboration has made the project more culturally and linguistically relevant and has helped youth understand Lions global causes in a very hands‑on way.

5. **Holistic Wellness and Multiple Global Causes**  
   While vision is the anchor, we intentionally layered in other global causes:
   - **Diabetes**: Every adult participant is offered a free blood glucose check and brief counseling. We identified 79 individuals with elevated readings suggestive of pre‑diabetes or diabetes, and 43 of them have since connected with a primary care provider or diabetes clinic.  
   - **Hunger Relief**: At each clinic, we set up a small “healthy pantry corner” with fresh produce (often rescued from local grocery overstock), whole grains, and simple recipes. We distributed more than 3,000 pounds of healthy food over the past year.  
   - **Environment**: We partnered with the city’s sustainability office to provide reusable shopping bags and information on walking paths and safe outdoor spaces, promoting physical activity and reduced plastic use.

6. **Data‑Driven Continuous Improvement**  
   Our club used MyLion reporting not just as a requirement, but as a planning tool. After the first three events, we analyzed participation numbers, demographic information, and follow‑up rates. We noticed that attendance from the Hispanic community was lower than expected, despite being a significant portion of the population in our east‑side neighborhoods. In response, we:
   - Shifted one monthly clinic to a Spanish‑speaking church.  
   - Ensured bilingual volunteers were present at every event.  
   - Translated all consent and educational materials into Spanish.  
   - Added evening hours to accommodate working families.

   This resulted in a 40% increase in participation from Hispanic residents in the subsequent six months.

7. **Exceeding Global Cause Goals**  
   Within one Lions year, we surpassed our club’s initial goals in multiple areas:
   - **Vision**: Our goal was to screen 500 individuals; we more than doubled that to 1,148.  
   - **Diabetes**: We aimed to provide diabetes risk information to 200 adults; we reached 381.  
   - **Environment**: Our goal was to distribute 250 reusable bags; we distributed 430, and we added a short educational segment on litter reduction and safe walking paths.  
   - **Hunger**: We initially planned to distribute 1,000 pounds of healthy food; we tripled that.

8. **Personalized, Dignified Service**  
   A key distinguishing feature is the way the service is delivered. We trained volunteers in trauma‑informed and culturally respectful communication, using a short training module developed with the county health department. Participants are greeted by name (if they choose to share it), offered a seat and water, and encouraged to ask questions without feeling rushed. One older participant, Mr. Alvarez, told us he had avoided doctors for years because of fear and cost. After coming to our event "just to check it out," he agreed to a screening, discovered he had very high blood sugar, and was connected to a community clinic. Two months later, he came back to thank us, sharing that he had started medication and felt more energetic and hopeful.

In summary, the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative is unique because it is not a one‑off project but a sustained, integrated program that meets people where they are, addresses multiple Lions global causes simultaneously, and uses data and partnerships to continually improve its reach and impact.

answers.value:
Riverbend is a mid‑sized town with a growing low‑income and immigrant population. While we have two hospitals, neither is easily accessible to residents in the east and south sides of town who lack reliable transportation or health insurance. School nurses and social workers repeatedly told us that students were struggling to see the board, adults were ignoring symptoms of diabetes, and many families had to choose between paying for prescription eyeglasses or paying their utility bills.

Before this project, there was no consistent, community‑based vision and basic wellness service in Riverbend. Occasional one‑day health fairs were held downtown, but they were sporadic, poorly advertised in certain neighborhoods, and often felt intimidating to residents who didn’t speak English fluently or lacked documentation. As a result, many people who needed help the most were not being reached.

The Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative has become important in our community for several concrete reasons:

1. **Early Detection and Prevention**  
   By providing regular, accessible screenings, we are catching vision and health issues earlier—often before they become crises. For example:
   - A 9‑year‑old student, Jasmine, was referred to us by her teacher because she kept squinting at the board and falling behind in reading. At our school‑based clinic, we discovered she had significant myopia. Through our referral network, she received an eye exam and glasses within three weeks at no cost to her family. Her teacher later reported a noticeable improvement in her reading engagement and confidence.  
   - At a clinic held at the Riverbend Food Pantry, we screened a 57‑year‑old man, Mr. Collins, who had blurry vision and frequent urination but had never seen a doctor due to cost. His blood glucose was critically high. We immediately connected him to a local free clinic; he was seen the next day and started on treatment. He later told us that the Lions clinic “probably saved his eyesight and maybe his life.”

2. **Reducing Disparities and Barriers to Care**  
   Our project specifically targets neighborhoods with lower income, higher unemployment, and higher proportions of residents for whom English is not a first language. We intentionally partner with trusted community institutions—schools, churches, housing authorities—so that participants feel safe and welcome.

   In surveys administered after our clinics (available in English and Spanish), many participants shared that they had never had their vision checked before, or that it had been more than five years since their last exam. Several undocumented residents told us they appreciated that our services did not require proof of insurance, immigration status, or a social security number.

3. **Supporting Children’s Education and Well‑Being**  
   Teachers and school administrators have repeatedly expressed gratitude for the program. One elementary school principal noted that, after our clinic, the number of students with updated vision referrals increased significantly, and several students who had been labeled as “distracted” or “unmotivated” were found to have uncorrected vision problems. By addressing vision issues, we are indirectly supporting literacy, classroom engagement, and overall educational outcomes.

4. **Strengthening Community Trust in Lions**  
   The consistent, visible presence of Lions in multiple neighborhoods has strengthened trust in our organization. Residents now recognize our yellow vests and often approach us at grocery stores or community events to ask when the next clinic will be held. This trust has led to more referrals, increased volunteer interest, and a deeper understanding of the Lions mission in Riverbend.

5. **Building a Bridge Between Residents and Healthcare Providers**  
   Many participants tell us they feel overwhelmed by the healthcare system—uncertain where to go, how much it will cost, or whether they will be treated respectfully. Our Lions volunteers serve as a bridge. For instance, when we help someone schedule an appointment, we provide a simple checklist: what to bring, what questions to ask, and how to follow up. For individuals who are nervous or have mobility challenges, one of our volunteers has even accompanied them to their first appointment when requested.

6. **Creating a Culture of Prevention and Self‑Advocacy**  
   Over time, we have seen a shift in attitudes. At first, many people came only because they “happened to be there” at the food pantry or school. Now, we have regulars who track the schedule and bring family members or neighbors. One grandmother, Mrs. Nguyen, came to our first clinic for a blood pressure check. She has since returned three times, each time bringing a different grandchild for a vision screening. She now keeps her own blood pressure log at home and proudly showed it to our volunteers, explaining that she feels more in control of her health.

7. **Quantifiable Community Impact**  
   In the past 12 months:
   - 1,148 individuals received at least one service (vision screening, blood glucose check, blood pressure check, or health education).  
   - 312 individuals were identified with potential vision issues; 184 have already received glasses through our partnerships, and 67 are in the process of scheduling or awaiting appointments.  
   - 79 individuals were identified with elevated blood glucose; 43 have confirmed follow‑up care.  
   - Over 3,000 pounds of healthy food were distributed, helping families who are both food‑insecure and at higher risk for diabetes and other chronic conditions.

8. **Resilience During Public Health Challenges**  
   Our initiative was designed with flexibility in mind, which proved essential during periods of increased respiratory illness in the community. We implemented masking and distancing protocols, moved portions of our education outdoors when weather permitted, and used appointment time slots to prevent crowding. This adaptability allowed us to continue serving residents safely when other events were being canceled.

9. **Empowering Volunteers and Future Leaders**  
   The project has energized our club and our partner Leo Club. Members who previously felt unsure about how to contribute now have clearly defined roles—setup, registration, data entry, education, referral follow‑up, translation, logistics. Several newer members have cited this project as the reason they joined and stayed with the Lions. Leos, in particular, have expressed pride in seeing their work directly help neighbors and classmates.

In sum, the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative is significant because it fills a critical gap in accessible, preventive health services; it reduces disparities for vulnerable populations; it supports children’s learning; and it strengthens the social fabric of our community by building trust, empowerment, and shared responsibility for health and well‑being.

This project is built on strong local partnerships that extend our reach and resources far beyond what our club could accomplish alone. Key partners and their contributions include:

1. **Riverbend Unified School District (RUSD)**  
   - **Role**: Host sites, outreach, and referrals.  
   - **Details**: RUSD allowed us to use multipurpose rooms and gymnasiums at two elementary schools (Lincoln Elementary and Eastside Elementary) for after‑school clinics at no cost. School nurses helped identify students who might benefit from screening and sent home bilingual flyers to families. RUSD’s communications office posted event information on the district website and social media pages, significantly increasing awareness.

2. **Riverbend Community Food Pantry**  
   - **Role**: Host site and connection to food‑insecure families.  
   - **Details**: The pantry offered its lobby and parking lot for monthly Saturday clinics. They also helped advertise the events to their clients via printed flyers and text message alerts. In return, we coordinated delivery of additional fresh produce and whole‑grain items from our club’s hunger relief partners on clinic days, making it easier for families to access both health services and nutritious food in one trip.

3. **Riverbend Vision Associates (Local Optometry Group)**  
   - **Role**: Professional follow‑up care and discounted services.  
   - **Details**: Three optometrists from Riverbend Vision Associates volunteered at least one clinic each quarter to oversee screenings and provide on‑site consultations for more complex cases. Their office agreed to provide comprehensive eye exams at a steeply discounted rate for referred low‑income residents and to donate or deeply discount frames and lenses for children in financial need. Over the past year, they have provided 97 free or low‑cost exams and 63 pairs of glasses as part of this partnership.

4. **St. Mark’s Community Church**  
   - **Role**: Host site and trusted community hub.  
   - **Details**: St. Mark’s, located in a predominantly Hispanic neighborhood, offered its fellowship hall for evening clinics every other month. The pastor and church volunteers helped with outreach in Spanish, encouraged congregants to attend, and provided light snacks and coffee. Their involvement greatly increased participation from families who previously had limited contact with formal health services.

5. **Riverbend Housing Authority**  
   - **Role**: Access to residents in public housing.  
   - **Details**: The Housing Authority allowed us to set up clinics in the community room of the Oak Terrace housing complex. They coordinated with their resident services coordinator to distribute information door‑to‑door, and they provided tables, chairs, and custodial support. This partnership helped us reach seniors and individuals with disabilities who would have had difficulty traveling to other locations.

6. **River County Health Department**  
   - **Role**: Training, supplies, and referral pathways.  
   - **Details**: The health department provided a half‑day training for our volunteers on proper screening techniques, infection control, cultural humility, and basic motivational interviewing. They also supplied disposable gloves, alcohol swabs, and some of the glucose testing strips. Importantly, they designated a staff member as our liaison, who helps us connect participants with the county’s low‑cost diabetes clinic, women’s health services, and immunization programs.

7. **Riverbend High School Leo Club**  
   - **Role**: Youth volunteers and cultural/linguistic support.  
   - **Details**: The Leo Club has been instrumental in designing youth‑friendly materials, translating flyers and consent forms into Spanish, and assisting with registration and logistics at events. Several Leos are bilingual and help interpret during screenings. They also recorded short educational videos (in English and Spanish) on eye health and healthy snacking that we show on a loop at clinics using a portable projector.

8. **Healthy Riverbend Coalition (Local Nonprofit Network)**  
   - **Role**: Coordination and resource sharing.  
   - **Details**: As a coalition of local nonprofits focused on health, housing, and food security, Healthy Riverbend helped us align our project with other local initiatives and avoid duplication. They featured our project in their monthly newsletter, connected us with additional volunteers, and helped us secure a small community grant that funded extra screening supplies.

9. **Riverbend Parks & Recreation Department**  
   - **Role**: Environmental and physical activity component.  
   - **Details**: Parks & Rec provided printed maps of local walking trails, information on free or low‑cost fitness classes, and tips for safe outdoor activity for people with diabetes or mobility issues. At some clinics, they staffed a table to sign participants up for beginner walking groups, reinforcing the environmental and physical activity aspects of Lions’ global causes.

10. **Local Grocery Co‑op (Riverbend Fresh Market)**  
   - **Role**: Healthy food and incentives.  
   - **Details**: Riverbend Fresh Market donated surplus produce and whole‑grain products for distribution at events, as well as reusable grocery bags. They also provided small incentive coupons (e.g., $5 off a purchase of fresh fruits and vegetables) for participants who completed both vision and glucose screenings, which encouraged follow‑through while supporting a local business.

These partnerships were not merely transactional; they were collaborative. We held quarterly coordination meetings with key partners to review attendance data, participant feedback, and emerging needs. This collaborative structure helped us refine clinic locations, adjust hours, and tailor educational materials to be more relevant and accessible to the communities we serve.

We intentionally leveraged multiple Lions Clubs International (LCI) resources to plan, implement, and strengthen the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative.

1. **LCI Service Toolkit and Global Causes Resources**  
   - We used the **Vision Project Planning Guide** from the LCI website as a foundational planning tool. It helped us define clear objectives, identify target populations, and think through logistics such as volunteer roles, equipment needs, and follow‑up procedures.  
   - We also consulted LCI’s **Diabetes Awareness and Action Toolkit** to incorporate diabetes risk assessment and education into our clinics. The toolkit’s sample risk assessment questions and guidance on culturally sensitive messaging were particularly helpful.

2. **MyLion® Reporting and Analytics**  
   - We reported each monthly clinic as a separate service activity in **MyLion**, documenting the number of people served, volunteer hours, and specific services provided (vision screenings, diabetes screenings, food distribution, environmental education).  
   - Over time, we used MyLion’s reporting features to track cumulative impact and identify patterns—for example, which locations and times of day yielded the greatest participation, and which populations were underrepresented. This data informed our decision to add evening clinics at St. Mark’s Church and to increase bilingual outreach.

3. **LCIF Grant Guidance (Informal Use)**  
   - While we did not apply for a full LCIF grant this year, we reviewed the **LCIF grant application guidelines** to understand best practices for budgeting, impact measurement, and sustainability. This helped us structure our local funding requests and partner agreements more effectively. The grant guidance templates also influenced the way we collected outcome data (e.g., tracking how many people received follow‑up care).

4. **LCI Marketing and Branding Resources**  
   - We used LCI’s **official logo files and branding guidelines** to create professional‑looking flyers, banners, and social media graphics. This not only ensured we were aligned with Lions branding standards but also increased credibility with partners and community members.  
   - We adapted sample press release templates from the LCI Public Relations resources to share information with local newspapers and radio stations about our kickoff event and major milestones (such as surpassing 1,000 individuals served).

5. **LCI Training Materials**  
   - Several of our club members, including myself as service chairperson, completed online training modules through the **LCI Learn** platform on topics such as “Leading Effective Service Projects” and “Serving Safely.” We incorporated key points from these modules into our volunteer orientation and pre‑event briefings, especially around volunteer safety, confidentiality, and respectful communication.

6. **District and Multiple District Support (LCI Structure)**  
   - Through our district’s Global Service Team (GST) coordinator, we accessed additional LCI materials and examples of successful vision and diabetes projects from other clubs. This peer learning, grounded in LCI’s global framework, helped us avoid common pitfalls and adopt proven strategies, such as using appointment windows to reduce wait times and burnout among volunteers.

7. **LCI Vision and Diabetes Awareness Materials**  
   - We printed and displayed LCI‑produced **posters and brochures** on eye health, diabetes risk factors, and healthy lifestyle tips (some downloaded directly from the LCI website in both English and Spanish). These materials complemented the custom handouts our club and Leo partners created, reinforcing key messages with clear, visually appealing content.

In summary, LCI resources were not just background references; they actively shaped our project design, volunteer training, data tracking, and public communication. By aligning closely with LCI’s global cause strategies and tools, we were able to build a project that is both locally responsive and globally connected.

answers.value:
As club service chairperson, I would like to add several points that may not be fully captured in the structured questions above but are important to understanding the heart and sustainability of this project.

1. **Personal Motivation and Club Commitment**  
   On a personal level, this initiative is very meaningful to me. My father lost much of his vision due to uncontrolled diabetes that went undiagnosed for years. Our family struggled to navigate the healthcare system, and I saw firsthand how lack of early detection and follow‑up can impact every aspect of life—work, independence, and emotional well‑being. That experience motivated me to join the Lions and, eventually, to propose this project to our club. Several other members have similar stories, whether related to vision loss, diabetes, or health inequities. This shared sense of purpose has helped sustain volunteer energy even when events are logistically challenging.

2. **Volunteer Experience and Growth**  
   Our club has 31 active members, and an average of 14–18 members volunteer at each clinic. Many have developed new skills—using screening equipment, coordinating logistics, handling data, and speaking with participants about sensitive health topics in a compassionate way. One of our newer members, a retired postal worker named George, was initially hesitant to volunteer because he felt he “didn’t know anything about healthcare.” After participating in a few clinics and shadowing more experienced volunteers, he now leads the registration and check‑in table and has become one of our most enthusiastic ambassadors, often recruiting his neighbors to attend.

3. **Safety, Privacy, and Ethics**  
   We take participant privacy and safety seriously. We do not collect social security numbers or immigration status. We use first names and last initials on our internal tracking sheets, and only one designated club member (a retired nurse) maintains the secure master list to support follow‑up calls. We obtained written consent (in English or Spanish) from participants before performing screenings or making referrals, and we clearly explain that we are volunteers, not a replacement for medical care.

4. **Sustainability and Future Plans**  
   We have already begun planning how to sustain and expand this project in the coming year:
   - We are exploring the possibility of purchasing a second portable auto‑refractor so that we can run two screening lines simultaneously at larger events.  
   - We are working with the River County Health Department to formalize our referral pathways through a simple memorandum of understanding, which will help ensure continuity even if individual staff members change.  
   - We plan to apply for a small LCIF grant next year to support equipment upgrades and expanded outreach to rural areas just outside Riverbend, where transportation barriers are even more severe.

5. **Stories that Illustrate Impact**  
   Beyond numbers, there are many individual stories that stay with us:
   - A single mother of three, Maria, came to a clinic at St. Mark’s Church after seeing a flyer in Spanish. She had been putting off getting her own eyes checked because she prioritized her children’s needs. At our clinic, she discovered she needed glasses and learned about her elevated blood pressure. With our help, she scheduled a follow‑up at the community clinic. Two months later, she returned to another event wearing her new glasses and told us she no longer had daily headaches and felt more confident at work.  
   - A high school student, Luis, volunteered through the Leo Club and initially saw this as a way to earn service hours. Over time, he became deeply engaged, particularly in helping older Spanish‑speaking participants feel comfortable. He has since expressed interest in pursuing a career in public health and has asked to help with data analysis and project planning next year.

6. **Recognition and Replicability**  
   While our primary focus is service, we have begun to receive local recognition. The Riverbend City Council invited our club to present about the project at a public meeting, and a local newspaper ran a feature story highlighting our partnership with the school district and food pantry. Several neighboring Lions clubs have reached out to ask how they might replicate a similar model in their communities. We have already shared our planning documents, sample flyers, and data tracking templates with two clubs in our district.

7. **Gratitude**  
   We are deeply grateful for the support of our partners, the flexibility and dedication of our volunteers, and the trust shown by community members who choose to participate in our clinics. Regardless of whether our club is selected for the Kindness Matters Service Award, this project has already transformed the way we serve and the relationships we have with our neighbors.

Thank you for considering the Riverbend Community Lions Club and the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative for this recognition. We believe it reflects not only the spirit of kindness but also the practical, sustained commitment to service that Lions around the world strive to embody every day.

persona.background_context:
This is Patricia Reynolds, a 47‑year‑old community health program coordinator living in Riverbend, Missouri, with her husband Mark and their two kids, Emily and Jacob. She’s deeply rooted in the town’s civic life and serves as the service chairperson for the Riverbend Community Lions Club. Her email—patricia.reynolds@riverbendlions.org—is on half the flyers around town because she’s the point person for the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative, a project she helped design and now coordinates month after month. Patricia’s weekdays start early: she’s usually up by 5:45 a.m., making coffee and scanning her calendar before waking the kids. Mark leaves around 7:15 for Riverbend High School, where he teaches U.S. history, while Patricia juggles getting Jacob to the bus stop and Emily to an early Leo Club meeting or band rehearsal. By 8:30 she’s at her office at the Riverbend Community Health Partnership, a small nonprofit that coordinates local health and social services. Her job blends naturally with her Lions work—she spends her mornings on calls with the River County Health Department, the Riverbend Unified School District, and the Riverbend Food Pantry, confirming dates and logistics for upcoming Mobile Vision & Wellness Days.

Much of Patricia’s current life revolves around sustaining and refining the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative. She spends one evening a week at home updating the secure follow‑up spreadsheet and preparing call lists for a retired nurse in the club who handles confidential outreach. On alternating Tuesdays, she meets with partners at Lincoln Elementary or the Riverbend Housing Authority to talk about turnout and what they’re hearing from residents. Her personal motivation is never far from her mind: her father, Thomas, lost much of his vision due to uncontrolled diabetes that went undiagnosed for years, and he passed away in 2020. Those memories show up quietly in her daily routine—she keeps a worn photo of him in her planner, and when she sees someone at a clinic with dangerously high blood sugar, she feels a mix of urgency and tenderness. At home, evenings are a balance of the ordinary and the mission‑driven. Jacob has soccer practice twice a week, Emily splits her time between homework, Leo projects, and texting Patricia photos of draft infographics for the next clinic. Dinner is often something simple—sheet‑pan chicken and vegetables, or a big pot of lentil soup—partly because Patricia is trying to model the healthy eating she talks about at clinics.

Recently, life has felt especially full. The initiative’s success has brought new requests: the city council asked Patricia to present about the program in January, and two neighboring Lions clubs have invited her to share how Riverbend built its mobile model. She squeezes these talks in between grant‑writing tasks at her nonprofit and parent‑teacher conferences for Jacob, who is adjusting to the heavier workload in 7th grade. Her mother, Carolyn, still lives an hour and a half away in Brookfield, and Patricia drives up at least once a month—usually on a Sunday—to take her grocery shopping and to her own eye appointments, mindful of the family’s history with diabetes. Patricia worries quietly about burnout, both for herself and her fellow Lions, so she has started intentionally delegating: George, a retired postal worker in the club, now runs the check‑in table at clinics, and Emily and her Leo friends handle much of the Spanish‑language flyer design. On a typical Saturday clinic day, Patricia is on her feet from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m., moving between stations, greeting participants by name when she remembers them, coordinating with the optometrists from Riverbend Vision Associates, and checking in with volunteers to make sure they’ve had a chance to sit and drink water.

Despite the long hours, Patricia feels a deep sense of satisfaction when she sees familiar faces returning to clinics with new glasses or improved lab results. She keeps a small notebook in her bag where she jots down stories—like Mr. Alvarez, who came back to thank the club after starting diabetes treatment, or Maria, a single mother who told Patricia she can finally work a full shift without a pounding headache now that she has glasses. These stories help her push through the more tedious parts of her week: endless emails, budget spreadsheets, and the occasional friction between partners about space or scheduling. She and Mark try to protect Friday nights as family time—pizza, a movie, and no laptops allowed—so that their home doesn’t feel like an extension of the clinic. As spring approaches, Patricia is thinking ahead: she wants to secure a small grant to buy a second portable auto‑refractor, and she’s exploring the idea of adding a quarterly clinic in a rural church just outside Riverbend. Her days are busy and sometimes exhausting, but they are coherent and purposeful, woven together by a simple goal that touches every part of her life: making sure that people in her community don’t have to go through what her father did, and that access to basic vision and wellness care is treated as a shared responsibility, not a privilege.

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I finally have a quiet half hour while Jacob is at soccer and Mark took Emily to band, so I wanted to write instead of just sending you another quick text.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Dad this week. I pulled out the old notebook where I kept all his endocrinology appointment notes from when he was going back and forth to the University of Missouri Health Care clinic in Columbia. Seeing those A1C numbers slide the wrong way and the dates he skipped follow‑ups because he didn’t have the gas money for the 120‑mile round trip brought it all back. I used those same notes back in January 2022 when I was sketching out the screening questions and follow‑up scripts for our Riverbend Vision & Wellness days, trying to catch people before they end up where he did.

It hit me again how many of his appointments at St. Luke’s in Kansas City and then in 2019 at St. Mary’s in Jefferson City got pushed or canceled over $40 of gas or an $85 copay. That’s exactly the kind of thing we’re seeing now at the clinics. On Saturday at the food pantry, an older gentleman told me he’d been rationing his metformin and skipping insulin doses since December because of a $620 bill at Riverbend Regional. We sat in the pantry’s back office for 45 minutes on the phone with billing and got them to set up a $25‑a‑month plan so he could refill his meds. I kept thinking, “I wish someone had done this with Dad.”

Day‑to‑day life is the usual chaos—Emily’s Leo Club projects, Jacob’s science fair, Mark grading AP essays at the kitchen table—but that through‑line of Dad’s story is always there under it all.

Love you,
Patricia

metadata.subject: Thinking about Dad and the clinics

artifacts.content:
Personal planning / brain dump – late night at the dining table

I keep circling back to Caleb from Lincoln Elementary. Back in October 2023, when we did that Mobile Vision & Wellness day at the school, his screening showed both severe myopia and a scary high blood sugar. I remember sitting in my office at Riverbend Community Health Partnership on October 17, calling his mom, walking her through the county charity‑care forms, and then driving with them to Children’s Mercy in Kansas City for that first pediatric endocrinology visit. Hearing the doctor say he was already in early‑stage type 2 diabetes at age nine was like watching Dad’s story replay in fast‑forward.

I want to use Caleb’s case, plus Jasmine at Eastside and Mr. Collins from the pantry, when we train new volunteers in January 2024—real stories that show why the integrated model matters. But I also need to be careful about details and privacy. Same issue with Maria from St. Mark’s whose retinal exam showed advanced diabetic retinopathy and who now has to go back and forth to St. Joseph’s in Kansas City for laser treatments over the next 6–8 months. We’re quietly lining up rides through the Lions, the church, and her sister.

Note to self: our diabetes/vision project honestly isn’t that “innovative” in terms of activities. We’re doing the same basic screenings and health‑fair type booths everyone does; what’s different is the persistence and the follow‑through.

Also: remember to check in with Emily about how she’s feeling using Caleb’s story in training, since he’s her neighbor and she’s been helping translate for his mom.

artifacts.content:
Hi Linda,

I wanted to jot down some thoughts while Saturday is still fresh in my mind.

The March 9 clinic at the Riverbend Food Pantry went well on paper—63 people screened in about four hours—but I keep replaying my conversation with Mr. Alvarez. When he sat down with me in the little back office and admitted he’d been rationing his metformin and skipping insulin since December 2023 because of that $620 bill at Riverbend Regional, my stomach dropped. Sitting on hold with the hospital billing department together and finally getting them to agree to a $25/month plan felt like a small miracle, but also like something volunteers shouldn’t have to improvise.

It reminded me of the Oak Terrace situation last year. Linda Chavez pulled me aside in May 2023 to tell me that Mr. Jenkins—the 63‑year‑old we’d screened in February—had been evicted in April after his vision deteriorated, he lost his deli job at Riverbend Fresh Market, and he fell behind on rent. You and I talked later at Healthy Riverbend about how we only heard about it after the eviction notice. We need a better way to flag people who are clearly sliding toward crisis.

I also keep thinking about Mom sitting in the waiting area at the February pantry clinic, quietly watching those 63 people cycle through, then admitting on the drive back to Brookfield that she skipped her own eye exam when her Medicare Part D premium jumped $47/month and the furnace died.

On the bright side, the volunteer team is getting smoother with setup and teardown, and the pantry staff were wonderful as always. But days like this make the work feel both crucial and impossibly small at the same time.

See you Thursday,
Patricia

artifacts.content:
Debrief – project design + data (written at home after kids’ bedtime)

I spent this afternoon pulling de‑identified numbers from MyLion and then cross‑checking them against the password‑protected spreadsheet on my home laptop—the one with the 287 names, phone numbers, and high‑risk flags for people we saw between July 2022 and June 2023. It dawned on me (again) that I’ve basically become the unofficial keeper of a quasi‑medical registry for Riverbend without any kind of formal HIPAA‑style agreement. I already emailed Greg to ask about better encryption and whether we should move the file off my personal machine.

While I was at it, I went back through the notes from May 3, 2023 with Lisa Turner at Riverbend Middle. That’s when she showed me her confidential list of 27 kids who failed vision screenings and never got follow‑up, including the family with the $326 balance at Riverbend Eye Care Associates. That conversation is what pushed me to start quarterly school‑based clinics in August 2023.

On the systems side, I keep meaning to use more of the official Lions resources—MyLion webinars, GST trainings, the global cause guides—but in reality I’ve mostly leaned on my public health background and just figured things out as we went. I never did sign up for those online reporting sessions; there’s always one more email from the county or a kid who needs a ride.

Action items: talk with Sarah (district GST) again about what absolutely must be in MyLion vs. what stays in our own files, and start drafting a simple data‑sharing and privacy protocol for the club.

metadata.title: Data + privacy reflections

artifacts.content:
Hi Greg,

Thanks again for always being my unofficial tech support.

I had a bit of a wake‑up call during our internal review last week. To make sense of our follow‑up rates, I exported the de‑identified counts from MyLion and then opened the master spreadsheet I keep on my personal laptop here at home. That file has 287 individual records from July 2022 through June 2023—names, phone numbers, screening results, and little flags for “possible diabetes,” “urgent vision referral,” etc. Between that and the notes I was tempted to put into MyLion (like the 62‑year‑old neighbor from Willow Bend whose blood sugar was over 300 at the February 17, 2024 St. Mark’s clinic), I realized I’m skating way too close to something that looks like a medical registry.

After my Zoom with Lion Sarah Mitchell on March 4, I went back into MyLion and scrubbed the identifiable details, but I’m still uneasy about the spreadsheet living on my personal laptop, even with a password. Can you help me think through encryption or a better storage option? Also, longer term, we probably need a simple data/privacy policy for the club so this isn’t just “whatever Patricia thinks is reasonable.”

Separate but related: when I look back, we honestly didn’t use much from LCI beyond downloading the logo that first year. I was so overwhelmed just getting the mobile days off the ground that I never had the bandwidth to dig into the toolkits or online trainings. If you’ve found any tech‑related LCI resources that are actually practical, I’m all ears.

Appreciate you,
Patricia

artifacts.content:
Late‑night reflection – why this still feels so hard sometimes

Some days I feel like the Riverbend Vision & Wellness project is the most meaningful thing I’ve ever helped build. Other days, it feels like we’re pushing a boulder up a hill that nobody asked us to climb.

When we first floated the idea, a lot of people in town shrugged. More than once I’ve heard some version of, “Sure, screenings are nice, but we’ve got bigger problems.” And they’re not wrong—families are juggling two or three jobs, caregiving, housing insecurity. What we’re doing is a tiny drop in a very leaky bucket.

Partnerships have been harder than I expected, too. We’ve had wonderful allies—Riverbend Vision Associates, the food pantry, St. Mark’s—but a couple of organizations turned us down flat early on because they couldn’t see what was in it for them. And the neighboring town’s Lions club, the one we tried to rope in that first year, bailed after we couldn’t agree on whose logo went where and who would get “credit.” It was so disheartening that we finally just moved ahead on our own.

Meanwhile, inside our own club it’s the same small core of us hauling tables and running registration while a few officers drift in just in time for the photo op. I know everyone is busy, but on nights like this, after the kids are in bed and my feet are still throbbing from a Saturday clinic, I catch myself wondering how long we can sustain this pace.

And yet—then I picture Caleb at Children’s Mercy, or Maria heading up to St. Joseph’s for her laser treatment, or Mom quietly watching 63 neighbors get screened at the pantry. That’s usually enough to get me to open the laptop and start planning the next one.

metadata.title: Why this feels both essential and small

artifacts.content:

Catching up between meetings and wanted to get some thoughts to you while they’re fresh.

I finally completed the LCI Learn “Serving Safely” module on January 9 from my office at Riverbend Community Health Partnership. On the home front, things are their usual juggling act. Emily is thriving in the Leo Club but still gets anxious around big presentations; I’m proud she stood up with me at the Riverbend City Council meeting on August 14, 2023 to talk about the clinics. Jacob is counting down to soccer season. Mom has another eye check in Brookfield next month, and I’ll tack it onto a grocery run like always.

Best,

artifacts.content:
End‑of‑year vent / reflection – not for sharing

Another long clinic season wrapped, and I’m exhausted in that bone‑deep way that doesn’t go away after one quiet weekend.

I keep thinking about March 2023, when I nearly passed out at the Riverbend Food Share clinic and wound up at Riverbend General Hospital overnight. Dr. Lawson adjusted my meds and told me point‑blank I couldn’t keep doing 50–60 volunteer hours a month on top of my full‑time job, at least not if I wanted to be around for Emily and Jacob. I’ve tried to stick closer to 25–30 hours since, but it still adds up.

Some of the discouragement is internal. Our general membership participation has been so hit‑or‑miss. It’s the same half‑dozen of us who show up early, stay late, and handle the messy parts, while a few officers seem to materialize only when there’s a newspaper photographer or a ribbon to cut. I don’t begrudge anyone a lighter season, but it’s hard not to feel resentful when my Saturdays disappear and I’m the one answering late‑night calls about a neighbor’s blood sugar.

And then there’s the reporting mess. We haven’t submitted half of what we’ve actually done into MyLion over the past few years. Between my own resistance to more screen time and the clunky interface, I’ve let a lot of events slide. On paper it probably looks like our club is barely doing anything, when in reality we’re running ourselves ragged.

Still, when I flip through my little story notebook—Caleb, Mr. Alvarez, Maria, Mom at the pantry—I remember why we started. Maybe next year the balance will feel a little saner.

metadata.title: End-of-year vent

artifacts.content:
From: Patricia Reynolds <patricia.reynolds@riverbendlions.org>
To: Self – grant writing folder (Draft narrative)
Date: 2024-01-22
Subject: DRAFT – Personal motivation & partnerships (do not send)

[Grant narrative scratchpad – for my eyes only]

When I talk about why the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative matters, I always come back to my dad, Thomas Hayes. Between January–August 2021 his vision declined so fast, and by the time he died from diabetes complications at Mercy Hospital in Brookfield, MO, I had a notebook full of the $85 copay eye appointments he canceled in Brookfield because he couldn’t get a ride or afford it. I still remember sitting with him at the St. Mary’s Hospital ophthalmology clinic in Jefferson City in June 2019, realizing how many chances we’d missed.

That’s why, when we launched monthly clinics on the east side of Riverbend in March 2022, I pushed hard for free diabetic retinopathy screenings and transportation vouchers for older adults. It’s also what led me, in March 2022, to meet privately with his endocrinologist, Dr. Karen Liu, at the River County Health Department low‑cost clinic. I asked if she’d advise our Lions project; now she comes to our quarterly coordination meetings at the Riverbend Community Health Partnership office and shares de‑identified trends so we don’t repeat Dad’s story.

On the youth side, Emily’s experience in September 2022 at Riverbend High School was the turning point. She failed her school vision screening, and at Dr. Carter’s office at Riverbend Vision Center we learned her headaches and slipping Algebra II grades were tied to significant astigmatism. After paying the $35 co‑pay for Emily’s exam, I vowed that every Mobile Vision & Wellness Day would include a pathway to same‑week optometry appointments. We piloted that referral system with Riverbend Vision Center in November 2022.

Cc: (draft – not yet sent)
Date: 2024-01-19
Subject: CONFIDENTIAL – coalition history + family intersections (notes)

Michael,

Drafting this for myself before I decide how much to actually share.

When you referenced the HRSA history at the Healthy Riverbend Coalition strategy meeting at the Riverbend Community Center on 1/18/24, it brought a lot back. In 2021 the HRSA program officer nearly pulled our three‑year grant for Riverbend Community Health Partnership over the perceived conflict between my grant‑funded coordinator role and my Lions Club Service Chair position. We ended up submitting a formal conflict management plan in March 2021 explaining how I separate my paid duties from the Lions clinics. That’s part of why the coalition now lists the Lions project as an external community partner instead of an internal program.

This work is tangled up with our family life too. In October 2022 my mom, Carolyn Hayes, had a minor car accident on Route 36 outside Brookfield after misjudging distance at night. Her ophthalmologist at Brookfield Eye Center later tied it to worsening diabetic retinopathy. Since then, I’ve been driving her the 68‑mile round trip to our Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach clinics at the Riverbend Community Center every other month for follow‑up screenings and transportation coordination, sometimes using my RCHP mileage reimbursement when Mark has late nights at Riverbend High School.

The only time we’ve ever canceled a clinic was May 6, 2023 at St. Mark’s Church in Riverbend, MO. I had to be at the Jackson County Courthouse in Kansas City that day over a contested bill from my father Thomas Hayes’s final three days in the ICU at Riverbend General Hospital, where he died from diabetes‑related complications. Because no one else was fully trained on the equipment and consent forms, we had to postpone. Hard lesson: we need at least two backup coordinators for every event.

Patricia

(Draft – not yet sent)

artifacts.content:
Google Doc – Personal reflection & story bank (Patricia M. Reynolds)
Last edited: 2024-07-03

Section: Youth & family stories I might reference (CONFIDENTIAL – do not share names publicly)

1) Emily’s screenings + Leo Club

Because Emily Reynolds is a junior at Riverbend High School and active in the Leo Club, a lot of this work feels close to home. In September 2023, her counselor, Ms. Rachel Ortiz, called about Emily’s anxiety after my mom, Carolyn Hayes, had a minor stroke and new vision changes. We met in Ms. Ortiz’s office at Riverbend High School to talk it through; that’s when I mentioned our Lions vision and glucose screening project. She suggested looping in the Riverbend High School Leo Club and shared (with Mom’s permission) that several students are caring for grandparents with diabetes complications, so we tailored the Leo partnership toward youth caregivers.

Emily’s own health has been a quiet driver too. In Spring 2023 she struggled with disordered eating and rapid weight loss. When I made an LCI‑branded flyer at home on Willow Bend Drive on 10/10/23, using the official LCI logo files and branding guidelines, I originally wrote that the project was inspired by her struggles. Mark Reynolds and I decided that was too personal to print on something we’d hand out at Riverbend High School and Riverbend Middle School, so I deleted that line before we printed 250 copies at the Riverbend Community Health Partnership office.

2) Classmate’s undocumented family

In October 2023, during a Leo Club meeting at Riverbend High, one of Emily’s 10th‑grade classmates told me her undocumented parents were avoiding eye care because they were afraid Riverbend General Hospital would ask for Social Security numbers and report them. At our November 4, 2023 pop‑up in the Riverbend Housing Authority community room, we screened both parents and found her father’s blood pressure was dangerously high. We linked him to the local free clinic’s sliding‑scale program and helped them complete intake without insurance or SSNs. Emily later said her friend told her, “Our family might actually be able to stay here and be okay.”

3) LCI tools + our own family

We leaned heavily on the LCI Diabetes Awareness and Action Toolkit after my dad, Thomas Hayes, lost most of his vision and died at St. Luke’s Hospital in Kansas City, MO in March 2021. I used the toolkit’s screening questions when I sat with Mom for her diabetes risk checklist at her primary care visit on April 12, 2023, then copied that checklist format into intake forms for our April–June 2024 Riverbend Vision & Wellness clinics at St. Mark’s Church.

4) Budget + Mark’s stress leave

For budgeting, I downloaded the LCIF grant application templates in August 2023 and used their sample budget lines almost verbatim for the $7,500 funding request we submitted to the Riverbend Community Hospital Foundation on September 5, 2023. In that proposal I noted that Mark had taken unpaid leave from Riverbend High School in May 2023 for stress‑related health issues, which meant we couldn’t personally float equipment costs and were relying more heavily on the LCIF‑style budget justification.

From: Patricia Reynolds <patricia.reynolds@riverbendlions.org>
To: Dr. Melissa Grant <mgrant@rivercountyhealth.mo.gov>
CC: Mark Reynolds <mreynolds@riverbendhs.org>, Emily Reynolds <emily.reynolds@student.riverbendhs.org>
Date: February 12, 2026
Subject: Endorsement letter & background details for Vision & Wellness initiative

Hi Melissa,

Thank you again for agreeing to write the endorsement for our Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative. The grant portal is asking me to confirm the endorsement date as 2026-02-20, which matches what you suggested for your clinic admin time next week, so I’ll plug that in unless you need me to adjust.

You asked for a few specifics that show how the project is uniquely youth‑driven. One key story is Emily’s experience in September 2022, when she failed her vision screening at Riverbend High School. When we took her to Dr. Carter’s office, we learned that her headaches and slipping grades in Algebra II were tied to significant astigmatism. That scare is what pushed me to insist that every Mobile Vision & Wellness Day include a pathway for students to get same‑week optometry appointments, which we piloted with Riverbend Vision Center in November 2022.

I also think the endorsement should be candid about our learning curve. In the first year we tried this, turnout was pretty low and we even had to cancel one of the planned screening days because only a handful of people signed up in advance. That experience is what led us to partner more intentionally with the Leo Club and the school counselors.

If it helps, I can drop by your office on the 19th with our latest data summary and a one‑page timeline. Otherwise, feel free to email me a draft and I’ll just confirm receipt for the application file.

With appreciation,
Patricia

Patricia Marie Reynolds
Community Health Program Coordinator
Riverbend Community Health Partnership
Service Chairperson, Riverbend Lions Club

Dear Ms. Reynolds,

Thank you for submitting the updated Vision & Wellness Outreach proposal and for attaching the draft endorsement letter from the Riverbend Community Health Partnership.

Before our board packet goes out, we need to confirm the timing of your executive director’s formal endorsement. The draft you sent references “our forthcoming endorsement in mid-March 2026,” but the cover email from your director mentioned that she hoped to sign no later than February 10, 2026 so it could be included with your LCIF-style budget narrative.

Because our trustees sometimes ask about alignment between grant decisions and partner endorsements, it would help to know the exact date you expect the endorsement letter to be signed and placed on letterhead. Best regards,

Subject: Re: Clarification on project date for nomination form

Thanks for your thoughtful questions and for sending the draft of your nomination. The Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative is exactly the kind of sustained project we like to see highlighted.

You can explain in the narrative section that you’re still catching up on service reporting and that you’ve been learning the GST processes as you go.

Hope this helps. Please send the final version once your club officers have signed.

In service,
Marla Jensen
District Governor, MD‑26
